bottles.desc: 100 proof. Single season. The 2025 release of Colonel E.H. Taylor Bottled-in-Bond — the most current annual edition of the expression that honors the man who lobbied for the federal standard it exemplifies. Colonel Edmund Haynes Taylor Jr. championed the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897 — the first federal consumer protection law in American spirits history. The BiB standard requires single distillery, single distilling season, minimum four years aging, exactly 100 proof, and federally bonded government-supervised warehouse production. Every E.H. Taylor Bottled-in-Bond is a direct tribute to thatâ¦

Taylor, Jr. Cured Oak is a 100-proof, Bottled-in-Bond bourbon aged ten years in barrels built from white oak staves air-dried for 13 months — more than double the standard six-month curing time. For E.H. Taylor devotees, Cured Oak is a deep cut: one of the most coveted bottlings in the lineup, absent for more than a decade. Originally released in 2015 with a 17-year age statement, it became a secondary-market grail commanding $3,999 or more. Its 2026 return arrives as a rare, limited offering rather than an annual release. What sets Cured Oak apart is the wood. Buffalo Traceâ¦

Standard bourbon uses three grains: corn (the majority), a secondary grain (rye or wheat), and malted barley. Colonel E.H. Taylor Four Grain adds a fourth — wheat alongside the corn, rye, and malted barley — creating a mashbill with no precedent in the standard Taylor lineup. bottles.desc: After serving in the Louisville Brigade during the Mexican-American war, William Larue Weller returned home to his native Kentucky. William Larue Weller returned from the Mexican-American War in the 1840s and joined his family's distilling business. In 1849, he introduced what would become one of bourbon's most significant innovations: a bourbon using wheat as its secondary grain rather than rye. Wheated bourbons — softer, sweeter, and more caramel-forward than rye-recipe bourbons — would go on to define the mostâ¦

Weller introduced the first bourbon to use wheat as its secondary grain instead of rye in 1849, giving wheated bourbons (including Pappy Van Winkle and Larceny, which later followed the Weller mashbill) their softer, gentler, slightly sweeter profile.
